figures.caption: Figure 2. Explanation sufficiency and necessity analysis for ReSS via feature masking across four tabular datasets, averaged over three random seeds. The x-axis denotes the number of masked features per instance, while the y-axis shows the resulting change in prediction accuracy under masking interventions.
Figure 3. An example of step-by-step reasoning curated by ReSS on AD dataset.

Figure 16. Sufficiency and necessity analysis via feature masking on Diabetes and AD. We compare the ReSS model trained without augmented reasoning data against the DRC+SFT baseline, whose reasoning data is generated without symbolic scaffold constraints.
Figure 8. An example of step-by-step reasoning obtained by direct reasoning curation on Creditg dataset.

Table 2. Classification accuracy (%) of different training methods across multiple datasets. Results are averaged over three random seeds for stochastic methods, with subscripts indicating the corresponding standard deviations. Decision Tree and XGBoost results on the AD dataset do not report standard deviations, as these methods are deterministic and only the official train–validation–test split is used.
Figure 7. An example of step-by-step reasoning obtained by direct reasoning curation on Alzheimer’s Disease dataset.
Figure 11. An example of delexicalized step-by-step reasoning curated by ReSS on the Diabetes dataset.
Figure 1. An illustration of the ReSS pipeline applied to the diabetes prediction problem.
Figure 12. An illustrative testing example from the Diabetes dataset.
Figure 10. An example of step-by-step reasoning obtained by direct reasoning curation on Homeloan dataset.
Figure 14. Direct RL vs. ReSS (w/o aug.) + RL. Figure 15. ReSS vs. ReSS (w/o aug.) + RL.
Figure 13. Ablation study with delexicalized features, conducted without augmented reasoning data. Results are averaged over three random seeds.
Figure 9. An example of step-by-step reasoning obtained by direct reasoning curation on Diabetes dataset.
